title: Introduction
content:
Local government reorganisation is being pursued at pace, framed publicly around simplification, stronger governance, and improved delivery. Experience from councils that have already undergone structural change shows that these outcomes are possible, but neither automatic nor evenly distributed.

This note draws on the experience of councils that have completed reorganisation within the past five years, including the creation of new unitary authorities from district and county structures. While each reorganisation reflects its own geography, politics and starting conditions, common patterns are now sufficiently clear to inform future design and decision making.

The purpose of this paper is not to argue for or against reorganisation in principle. It is to set out what recent experience tells us, and what future programmes must do differently if the stated benefits of reform are to be realised.
